relationship.qualifier: silvopasture reduces grass biomass, litter, and duff depth, thereby reducing fuel loads and wildfire risk; measured at two Washington State ponderosa pine sites

EV_evidence_register.quote: This study compared forest composition and structure, fuel types, and vegetative biomass between silvopasture and non-grazed managed forests in Washington, U.S. We show that silvopasture management results in reductions in grass biomass, litter, and duff depth when compared to non-grazed managed forest. These findings point to the integrated nature of silvopasture, where management of overstory composition and structure, understory vegetation, and grazing can reduce fuel loads and potential wildfire risk.
EV_evidence_register.raw_name: fuel loads and potential wildfire risk
EV_evidence_register.attribution: Temperate-US scope (Washington State dryland ponderosa pine forest); Mediterranean studies referenced but not the primary site. Findings may not transfer directly to tropical/subtropical or humid-temperate silvopastoral systems.

relationship.qualifier: potential (not measured) reduction in fire frequency — reduced overstory density and spatial variability vs other land uses; epistemic claim only

EV_evidence_register.quote: The reduced density and increased spatial variability of the overstory, combined with other silvopasture management practices, has the potential of reducing fire frequency when compared to other land uses.
EV_evidence_register.attribution: [VERIFY-FLAG mismatch: quote says "has the potential of reducing fire frequency" (conditional/hypothetical); direction field asserts "negative" with no hedge — overstates certainty of an empirical claim.] Temperate-US paper; claim cites Mediterranean/other research (ref 32). Mechanism is overstory structure management.

relationship.qualifier: rotational grazing removes understory biomass, reducing herbaceous surface fuel loads

EV_evidence_register.quote: For livestock, use of rotational grazing in a silvopasture has been found to effectively remove understory biomass, thus reducing herbaceous surface fuel loads.
EV_evidence_register.attribution: Temperate-US paper; claim cites Mediterranean silvopasture research (refs 28, 33). Mechanism: rotational grazing forage consumption.

relationship.qualifier: litter and duff depth significantly lower in silvopasture vs non-grazed managed forest at both Washington State study sites (statistical significance reported but p-value not quoted)

EV_evidence_register.quote: Litter and duff depth were significantly lower at both silvopasture sites than the non-grazed managed forests (Table 4). One contributing factor may be due to the significant reduction of grass biomass from livestock grazing at silvopasture sites when compared to non-grazed managed forest sites (Table 5).
EV_evidence_register.attribution: [VERIFY-FLAG mismatch: qualifier states p<=0.05 but the quote only says "significantly lower" — no p-value is stated in the quoted text; this numeric precision is fabricated.] Primary measured data from two Washington State ponderosa pine silvopasture sites; temperate-US scope. Statistical significance at p<=0.05.

relationship.qualifier: litter and duff depth positively correlated with rate of fire spread and burn severity in this forest type

EV_evidence_register.quote: Reducing litter and duff has important implications for fire management. There is a correlation between rate of fire spread and burn severity with depth of litter and duff, particularly in this forest type.
EV_evidence_register.raw_name: rate of fire spread and burn severity
EV_evidence_register.attribution: [VERIFY-FLAG mismatch: qualifier appends an inference ("reducing litter/duff therefore reduces fire spread and severity") not explicitly stated in the quote; quote only asserts a correlation between depth and fire spread/severity.] Temperate-US paper (dryland ponderosa pine, Washington State); cite ref 51. Mechanism links litter/duff depth to fire behaviour.

relationship.qualifier: silvopasture reduces litter, duff, and herbaceous biomass (select fuel types) but not coarse woody debris (1h-1000h fuels) vs managed forest; Washington State, Interior Columbia Basin

EV_evidence_register.quote: Our research offers evidence that silvopasture reduces select fuel types and select vegetative biomass when compared to managed forests of the Interior Columbia Basin. However, 1-, 10-, 100-, and 1000-h fuels were similar between the management systems.
EV_evidence_register.raw_name: select fuel types and select vegetative biomass
EV_evidence_register.attribution: Primary measured data from Washington State ponderosa pine sites (Interior Columbia Basin); temperate-US scope. Important nuance: coarse woody fuels (1h-1000h) NOT significantly reduced.

EV_evidence_register.quote: Because of the more deliberate and systems approach to management, silvopastures are capable of improving ecosystem services, such as carbon storage, nutrient cycling, water infiltration rates, and improved biodiversity.
EV_evidence_register.attribution: Temperate-US paper; claim cites ref 26 (Smith et al. 2022 systematic review). Not quantified in this study; listed as ecosystem service of silvopasture generally. Low confidence - no primary data for this variable in this paper.

EV_evidence_register.quote: Adopting agroforestry has been proposed as one livelihood activity that can help build livelihood resilience to floods and droughts (Kandji et al. 2006, Verchot et al. 2007, Garrity et al. 2010, Lin 2011, Thorlakson and Neufeldt 2012, Bryan et al 2013, Simelton et al. 2015).
EV_evidence_register.attribution: Kandji et al. 2006; Verchot et al. 2007; Garrity et al. 2010; Lin 2011; Thorlakson and Neufeldt 2012; Bryan et al 2013; Simelton et al. 2015

context.mitigation_mechanism: deep root systems access greater soil volume for water and nutrients

EV_evidence_register.quote: Some of the ecological characteristics of agroforestry species make them resilient to floods and droughts, including deep root systems that are able to use a greater soil volume for water and nutrients (Kandji et al. 2006, Verchot et al. 2007).
EV_evidence_register.attribution: Kandji et al. 2006; Verchot et al. 2007

context.mitigation_mechanism: shade trees produce microclimates that buffer temperature fluctuations, reducing evapotranspiration

EV_evidence_register.quote: Shade trees can produce microclimates that buffer temperature fluctuations (Lin 2007), which in turn can reduce evapotranspiration.
EV_evidence_register.attribution: Lin 2007

EV_evidence_register.quote: Some of the ecological characteristics of agroforestry species make them resilient to floods and droughts, including deep root systems that are able to use a greater soil volume for water and nutrients (Kandji et al. 2006, Verchot et al. 2007). Shade trees can produce microclimates that buffer temperature fluctuations (Lin 2007), which in turn can reduce evapotranspiration. Further, trees have the ability to buffer crops from storms (Philpott et al. 2008).
EV_evidence_register.attribution: Philpott et al. 2008

context.note: Perception-based measure; effect not found in Kinna

EV_evidence_register.quote: Perceptions of changes in drought (Fig. 2) varied between communities and households that did and did not practice agroforestry. In Kinna, responses did not vary between households with and without agroforestry. However, for Burat there was a correlation between practicing agroforestry and perception of drought (χ2 = 13.7896, P < 0.01). Households practicing agroforestry largely responded that droughts were less frequent (64 respondents), whereas most households (46 respondents) without agroforestry stated the opposite.

context.note: Pareto front endpoints.

EV_evidence_register.quote: The Pareto-optimal scenarios formed a distinct curve, showing that scenarios delivering the highest mean sequestration change (up to ~2.5 Mg CO2 ha−1 year−1) are limited to very small, highly suitable AOI. Conversely, scenarios achieving the highest total sequestration (approaching 107 Mg CO2 year−1) do so by incorporating vast land areas, which drives the mean sequestration rate down to ~0.5 Mg CO2 ha−1 year−1.

context.note: Cumulative stock change — NOT annual rate.

EV_evidence_register.quote: The mean total sequestration change due to vegetation, SOC, and livestock together was approximately 7 Mg CO2 ha−1, with a standard deviation of 13.42 Mg CO2 ha−1 for vegetation, 3.66 Mg CO2 ha−1 for SOC, and 0.05 Mg CO2 ha−1 for livestock manure.

The estimated annual sequestration rates in our high-potential silvopastoral areas (≈0.5–2.5 Mg CO2 ha−1 year−1, equivalent to 0.14–0.68 Mg C ha−1 year−1) are consistent with the lower-to-mid range of values observed in Mediterranean agroforestry systems.

tree–pasture systems generally fall between 0.35 and 1.8 Mg C ha−1 year−1, depending on stand age and management intensity [91]. Meta-analyses report similarly broad variability, from modest rates of about 0.24 Mg C ha−1 year−1 in older European agroforestry systems to peaks near 1.5 Mg C ha−1 year−1 under improved management in Andalucian olive groves [92]. Higher values, up to 2 Mg C ha−1 year−1, have been documented for almond and vineyard systems with dense cover crops, and even 5.3 Mg C ha−1 year−1 where organic amendments were applied [93].
EV_evidence_register.attribution: Funes et al. 2022 (ref 91) — field measurements, Mediterranean woody crops, NE Spain

EV_evidence_register.quote: Meta-analyses report similarly broad variability, from modest rates of about 0.24 Mg C ha−1 year−1 in older European agroforestry systems to peaks near 1.5 Mg C ha−1 year−1 under improved management in Andalucian olive groves [92].
EV_evidence_register.attribution: Carranca et al. 2022 (ref 92) — meta-analysis, Mediterranean agroforestry, European focus

EV_evidence_register.quote: Recent research on Iberian dehesa systems aligns with this upper range, reporting total carbon stocks of 25–75 Mg C ha−1 and confirming the superior sequestration in wooded versus non-wooded shrublands [94].
EV_evidence_register.attribution: González-Morales et al. 2025 (ref 94) — Iberian dehesa systems

context.note: Co-benefits: biodiversity, water regulation, erosion, farm resilience.

EV_evidence_register.quote: Third, the scope of our optimisation is currently limited to a single objective: carbon sequestration. While this is a primary goal of climate policy, the true value of silvopastoralism lies in its multifunctionality, delivering co-benefits such as enhanced biodiversity, improved water regulation, reduced soil erosion, and increased farm resilience [39,122].
EV_evidence_register.attribution: Varela et al. 2022 (ref 39); Amorim et al. 2023 (ref 122)

context.note: Abstract: silvopastoral on marginal lands avoids food-security conflict.

EV_evidence_register.quote: Notably, converting arable land was not a primary contributor to carbon gains, as the marginal carbon benefit on these productive soils is lower than on marginal lands due to their higher baseline soil carbon levels, indicating that large-scale implementation can focus on marginal lands to avoid conflicts with food security.

context.note: Trade-off: carbon-centric strategies may conflict with wildfire prevention.

EV_evidence_register.quote: Expert-based assessments of Mediterranean systems confirm that such trade-offs are inherent, revealing, for example, potential conflicts between carbon storage and other services like wildfire prevention [124].
EV_evidence_register.attribution: Lecegui et al. (ref 124)

EV_evidence_register.quote: The ideal soil conditions for mulberry production include a soil depth ranging from 75 to 100 cm and a pH level between 6.5 and 7.5 (S1).

Mulberry agroforestry has the ability to withstand both high soil alkalinity and acidity, as mentioned in Bera (2002). It can thrive in soils that are moderately alkaline or acidic. However, for optimal mulberry growth, a soil pH range of 6.5 to 8.5 is considered ideal.

relationship.direction: temperature variables hold greater significance than rainfall variables in determining species distributions

EV_evidence_register.quote: Bio1 (Mean Annual Temperature): This variable is widely recognized as a strong predictor in species distribution models [33]. It provides crucial information about the overall thermal conditions experienced by tree species throughout the year. Additionally, Bio1, along with Bio12, is considered highly important in larger ecological studies. However, research indicates that temperature variables often hold greater significance than rainfall variables in determining species distributions [34].
EV_evidence_register.attribution: [VERIFY-FLAG scope: methods — Variable-selection justification explaining why Bio1 is a good predictor in species distribution models and that temperature variables outwe] citing refs [33] and [34] — not identified by name in this paper

relationship.direction: rainfall distribution critical for grouping tree species by water requirements

EV_evidence_register.quote: Bio12 (Mean Annual Precipitation): This variable was included in the suitability mapping process because rainfall distribution is crucial for grouping tree species based on their water requirements and ecological preferences. Bio12 represents the mean annual precipitation, providing essential data on the amount of rainfall received by a region annually. In similar dry deciduous regions, such as those in Sub-Saharan Africa, Bio12 has been identified as a critical variable influencing the distribution and ecological niche of tree species [35].
EV_evidence_register.attribution: [VERIFY-FLAG scope: methods — Variable-inclusion rationale for Bio12 (why rainfall distribution matters for grouping species). Methods/justification text with no precipit] citing ref [35] — dry deciduous Sub-Saharan Africa finding

EV_evidence_register.quote: Bulk Density: Bulk density is a crucial variable included in the model to assess soil compaction, drainage conditions, and overall soil quality [31]. It provides insights into the density and porosity of the soil, influencing root growth, water movement, and nutrient availability for tree species.
EV_evidence_register.attribution: [VERIFY-FLAG unsupported: Quote is narrative-only: describes bulk density as a proxy for drainage/compaction/porosity but states no numeric breakpoints, no suitability scores, and no AHP weight. All quantitative values in the relationship (0-134/134-144/144-146/146-200 breakpoints, suitability labels, AHP_weight 0.064) are absent from this quote and smuggled from elsewhere.] general agroforestry tree species for Punjab — applies cross-family

relationship.direction: positive — all services associated with agroforestry / tree plantation

EV_evidence_register.quote: Previous DSSs for tree selection have focused on various ecosystem services, including urban heat island (UHI) mitigation (23), air quality improvement (29), runoff reduction and erosion control (26), non-timber forest products (NTFPs) (28), timber production (30), water quality enhancement (19), slope protection (25), and biodiversity and cultural services (27).
EV_evidence_register.attribution: citing refs 19,23,25,26,27,28,29,30 — various tree DSS studies; review by same group [6]

EV_evidence_register.quote: By ensuring informed decisions, agroforestry can increase ecosystem service benefits, including nutrient enrichment, soil erosion control, and soil quality improvement [7].

relationship.note: shade cover >50% not recommended (N) for both Arabica (a) and Robusta (r); shade cover ≤50% recommended (Y); hard upper threshold in multicriteria decision aid

EV_evidence_register.quote: Shade cover > 50% Na,r Competition for light, reduced yields, increased pest and disease incidence ≤ 50% Ya,r Buffer against extreme events, stabilizing production

relationship.note: 25% shading → −2°C max, +0.5°C min; 50% shading → −4°C max, +1°C min; extrapolated from Sarmiento-Soler et al. 2019 experimental data for Cordia africana Uganda

EV_evidence_register.quote: To mimic a 25% and 50% shading effect, the monthly maximum temperature files were adjusted by subtracting 2 °C and 4 °C while adding 0.5 °C and 1 °C on the minimum temperature files, respectively as extrapolated from experimental microclimate regulation results for Cordia africana in Uganda (Sarmiento-Soler et al. 2019).
EV_evidence_register.attribution: Sarmiento-Soler et al. 2019

relationship.note: shading reduces max temperature by up to 4°C vs open sun; cited secondary aggregating multiple sources

EV_evidence_register.quote: Shading in agroforestry can reduce the average maximum temperature by up to 4 °C compared to open sun systems (Charbonnier et al. 2017; Merle et al. 2022; Moreira et al. 2018; Muschler 2001; Soto-Pinto et al. 2000).
EV_evidence_register.attribution: Charbonnier et al. 2017; Merle et al. 2022; Moreira et al. 2018; Muschler 2001; Soto-Pinto et al. 2000

EV_evidence_register.quote: Temperature seasonality contributes significantly, with a weight of 60% to the overall suitability model.

Robusta coffee is suitable in a relatively larger area representing 70% of the total land area. The species is highly suitable in lowlands below 1500masl (Fig. 4c), mostly the country's central and northwestern parts.

However, for Robusta coffee, both precipitation and temperature-related variables are essential for its suitability. Precipitation-related variables contribute more (42%) than temperature-related variables (30%).

Though precipitation variables contribute most (approximately 45%) to the suitability of banana, the individual contribution of soil pH and elevation is also high (24 and 13%), respectively (Fig. SI3).
Soil pH is equally vital in the suitability of Robusta coffee, contributing 15% to the overall suitability model.
EV_evidence_register.attribution: Hengl et al. 2015

EV_evidence_register.quote: Though precipitation variables contribute most (approximately 45%) to the suitability of banana, the individual contribution of soil pH and elevation is also high (24 and 13%), respectively (Fig. SI3).
EV_evidence_register.attribution: Hengl et al. 2015

EV_evidence_register.quote: The ideal pH of coffee plants usually ranges between 6 and 6.5. The data show that the pH of Patokpicis is 6.9, slightly above the ideal range; the pH of Klakah and Bangsri are 6.6 and 6.8, respectively, still within tolerance limits. Under the best conditions, Sumbersuko's pH is 6.2.

relationship.note: observed range across hamlets, all rated 'good' for coffee growth

EV_evidence_register.quote: All sites showed a good soil pH for coffee plant growth, with a pH between 6.2 and 6.9, which is ideal for many plants, and an average pH of 6.625, indicating that overall, the soils at these sites are quite favourable for agriculture.

context.note: Paper maps agricultural parcels (temporary crops dominant, grasslands secondary) as the host system for MF/AF introduction. No hard LULC exclusion threshold — selection required >=50% overlap with pressure raster. GIS criteria defined externally in Gabourel-Landaverde et al. (2025), not reproduced here.

EV_evidence_register.quote: The corresponding LULC classes within these refined target parcels were then mapped. The GIS-based landscape suitability assessment was carried out for the municipalities of the six co-design pilots and the ones surrounding them. These areas were selected based on an assessment identifying the highest environmental and climate change pressures.

context.note: FAMILY-MISFILE FLAG: Cardinael et al. (2017) studied silvoarable (crop+tree) systems, not silvopastoral (crop+livestock+tree). Filed under silvopastoral because the host source is mantino_2026 which covers both MF and AF. Central review should consider re-assigning to agroforestry__planted_silvoarable.

EV_evidence_register.quote: Cardinael et al. (2017), through a field study across six sites in France, demonstrated that in silvoarable systems, the mean soil organic carbon (SOC) stock accumulation rate was 0.24 (0.09–0.46) Mg C ha⁻1 yr⁻1 in the top 30 cm of soil, and 0.65 (0.004–1.85) Mg C ha⁻1 yr⁻1 in tree biomass. Increased SOC stocks were also found in deeper soil layers at two of the silvoarable sites.
EV_evidence_register.raw_name: soil organic carbon (SOC) stock accumulation rate
EV_evidence_register.observed_dataset: field measurements, 6 sites in France (Cardinael et al. 2017)
EV_evidence_register.attribution: Cardinael et al. (2017)

EV_evidence_register.quote: In the Mediterranean, trees can reduce heat stress in steers and heifers, resulting in up to a 20% increase in weight gain (Ripamonti et al. 2025a). Similarly, tree canopy shade has been shown to reduce heat stress in sheep (Turini et al. 2025).
EV_evidence_register.attribution: Ripamonti et al. (2025a); Turini et al. (2025)

EV_evidence_register.quote:
AGROFORESTERÍA CON CULTIVOS ANUALES (Aa):
Areas con limitaciones de pendiente y/o profundidad efectiva del
suelo, donde se permite la siembra de cultivos agrícolas asocia-
dos con árboles y/o con obras de conservación de suelos y prác-
ticas o técnicas agronómicas de cultivo. (English: AGROFORESTRY WITH ANNUAL CROPS (Aa): Areas with limitations of slope and/or effective soil depth, where planting of agricultural crops associated with trees and/or soil conservation works and agronomic practices/techniques is permitted.)
EV_evidence_register.attribution: [dropped 2026-06-23 by peetmate; reason:other; note:difficult to udnerstand the context, poor quality publication.] INAB (Instituto Nacional de Bosques, Guatemala) — methodology adapted from T.C. Sheng / Michaelsen

EV_evidence_register.quote:
AGROFORESTERÍA CON CULTIVOS ANUALES (Aa):
Areas con limitaciones de pendiente y/o profundidad efectiva del

dos con árboles y/o con obras de conservación de suelos y prác-

EV_evidence_register.attribution: [dropped 2026-06-24 by peetmate; reason:constrained_aoi; note:INAB Guatemala land-capability classification assigns agroforestry as a residual/marginal-land bucket (what's left after prime agriculture + protection forest) — understates where AF is feasible + constrains the AOI. Down-graded per Pete 2026-06-24; not used for opportunity-space extent.] [reviewed 2026-06-23 by peetmate] INAB (Instituto Nacional de Bosques, Guatemala) — methodology adapted from T.C. Sheng / Michaelsen

EV_evidence_register.quote:
AGROFORESTERÍA CON CULTIVOS PERMANENTES (Ap):
Areas con limitaciones de pendiente y profundidad, aptas para el
establecimiento de sistemas de cultivos permanentes asociados
con árboles (aislados, en bloques o plantaciones, ya sean espe-
cies frutales y otras con fines de producción de madera y otros
productos forestales). (English: AGROFORESTRY WITH PERMANENT CROPS (Ap): Areas with limitations of slope and depth, suitable for the establishment of permanent crop systems associated with trees (isolated, in blocks or plantations, whether fruit species and others for purposes of timber and other forest product production).)
EV_evidence_register.attribution: [dropped 2026-06-24 by peetmate; reason:constrained_aoi; note:INAB Guatemala land-capability classification assigns agroforestry as a residual/marginal-land bucket (what's left after prime agriculture + protection forest) — understates where AF is feasible + constrains the AOI. EV_evidence_register.quote:
SISTEMAS SILVOPASTORILES (Ss):
Areas con limitaciones de pendiente y/o profundidad, drenaje in-
terno que tienen limitaciones permanentes o transitorias de
pedregosidad y/o drenaje.  Permiten el desarrollo de pastos na-
turales o  cultivados y/o asociados con especies arbóreas. (English: SILVOPASTORAL SYSTEMS (Ss): Areas with limitations of slope and/or depth, internal drainage that have permanent or transitory limitations of stoniness and/or drainage. They permit the development of natural or cultivated pastures and/or those associated with tree species.)

FAM_family_registry.name: Alley cropping
FAM_family_registry.definition: Planted rows of woody perennials with annual crops cultivated in the alleys between them.
FAM_family_registry.dominant_limiting_factor: Arable biophysical envelope + machinery/management access on cultivable land.
FAM_family_registry.grouping_rationale: Planted silvoarable systems on cultivable land share a slope/soil/rainfall envelope and a management-access constraint; grouped by that shared limiting factor, not by tree species.

FAM_family_registry.name: Tree intercropping
FAM_family_registry.definition: Planted trees dispersed within or among annual crops on cultivable land.
FAM_family_registry.dominant_limiting_factor: Arable biophysical envelope + management access on cultivable land.
FAM_family_registry.grouping_rationale: Same planted-on-cropland envelope as alley cropping; same dominant limiting factor.

FAM_family_registry.name: Planted boundary / live fence on cropland
FAM_family_registry.definition: Deliberately planted tree/shrub lines on the boundaries of cultivated fields.
FAM_family_registry.dominant_limiting_factor: Arable biophysical envelope + management access; established by planting.
FAM_family_registry.grouping_rationale: Planted establishment on cropland places it in the silvoarable envelope family (distinct from F4, where the limiting factor is linear placement geometry rather than the arable envelope).

FAM_family_registry.name: Farmer-managed natural regeneration (FMNR)
FAM_family_registry.definition: Re-establishing tree cover by protecting and managing natural regrowth from living stumps, roots and seed, rather than planting.
FAM_family_registry.dominant_limiting_factor: Regeneration potential — remnant rootstock, seedbank, seed-source proximity, and tenure security to protect regrowth.
FAM_family_registry.grouping_rationale: Limited by regeneration potential, not the planting envelope; a different variable set (woody-cover baseline/change, seed-source distance, tenure), so it cannot be an override of F1.

FAM_family_registry.name: Parkland tree retention
FAM_family_registry.definition: Retaining/regenerating scattered mature trees on cropland (e.g. Faidherbia parkland) by protecting natural regeneration.
FAM_family_registry.dominant_limiting_factor: Regeneration potential + tenure (retention/ANR), not deliberate planting.
FAM_family_registry.grouping_rationale: Retained parkland is regeneration-driven (kin to ANR/FMNR); a planted parkland would instead sit in F1. Keyed by establishment driver, not appearance.

FAM_family_registry.name: Assisted natural regeneration on farmland
FAM_family_registry.definition: Active assistance (weeding, protection, thinning) to accelerate natural regeneration on farmland.
FAM_family_registry.dominant_limiting_factor: Regeneration potential — seedbank/rootstock, seed source, tenure.
FAM_family_registry.grouping_rationale: Same regeneration-potential limiting factor as FMNR and parkland retention.

FAM_family_registry.name: Planted silvopasture
FAM_family_registry.definition: Deliberate integration of planted trees with pasture and grazing livestock.
FAM_family_registry.dominant_limiting_factor: Biophysical envelope PLUS grazing context and forage/livestock variables.
FAM_family_registry.grouping_rationale: Needs additional variables (forage, grazing pressure, livestock) beyond the silvoarable envelope; overrides only re-parameterise existing variables, so it is its own family.

FAM_family_registry.name: Agrosilvopasture
FAM_family_registry.definition: Combined trees + crops + livestock on the same land unit.
FAM_family_registry.grouping_rationale: Shares the grazing-context limiting factor with planted silvopasture.

FAM_family_registry.name: Windbreaks & shelterbelts
FAM_family_registry.definition: Tree/shrub lines positioned to reduce wind exposure across fields.
FAM_family_registry.dominant_limiting_factor: Linear-feature geometry — wind exposure and field-boundary placement.
FAM_family_registry.grouping_rationale: Placement (line within a candidate zone) is the limiting logic, not arable envelope; footprint reported as candidate length/density, never pixel area.

FAM_family_registry.name: Contour & vegetative buffers
FAM_family_registry.definition: Tree/shrub lines along contours or slope-flow lines to reduce erosion and runoff.
FAM_family_registry.dominant_limiting_factor: Linear-feature geometry — slope/flow lines + boundary placement.
FAM_family_registry.grouping_rationale: Same linear-placement limiting factor as windbreaks; candidate length/density footprint.

FAM_family_registry.name: Shaded perennial-crop system
FAM_family_registry.definition: Shade trees grown over an existing perennial cash crop. The understorey crop (coffee, cocoa, tea, cardamom, pepper, ...) is a PARAMETER of the family, scoped per AOI - not a separate subpractice.
FAM_family_registry.dominant_limiting_factor: The host crop's observed distribution / production (where the crop is actually grown), plus shade-as-adaptation at the climate-stressed edge.
FAM_family_registry.grouping_rationale: One family, crop as a parameter (run per candidate crop, combine by max retaining the driving crop) - the way F1 is not split by tree species. Gated by the crop's observed distribution (MapSPAM / GAEZ / EO commodity maps / ag-stats), not a modelled niche; bioclimatic-envelope modelling is the fallback only where distribution data are absent. Crops are not blended into one envelope (coffee highlands and cocoa lowlands are nearly disjoint).

FAM_family_registry.name: Multistrata homegardens
FAM_family_registry.definition: Intensive multistrata tree-crop-livestock gardens adjacent to dwellings.
FAM_family_registry.dominant_limiting_factor: Settlement and water proximity at sub-pixel scale.
FAM_family_registry.grouping_rationale: Sub-pixel and proximity-driven; a raster suitability surface misleads, so handled qualitatively via the scorecard + M6 note rather than mapped.

FAM_family_registry.name: Macro-catchment water harvesting
FAM_family_registry.definition: Structures that collect runoff from a catchment larger than the cultivated/storage area (e.g. check dams, farm ponds).
FAM_family_registry.dominant_limiting_factor: Runoff generation + storable terrain (slope, drainage, soil permeability).
FAM_family_registry.grouping_rationale: Runoff-catchment WH practices share a runoff-generation + storage-terrain limiting factor; reported as an applicability zone, not pixel area.

FAM_family_registry.name: Cross-cutting envelope (family-agnostic)
FAM_family_registry.definition: Tagging value for evidence applying across ALL agroforestry families (e.g. general biophysical envelope claims), not one subpractice. Not a real subpractice.
FAM_family_registry.grouping_rationale: Meta-tag (v0.3.2, pending ratification) so general envelope evidence is not force-pinned to F1; see methodology/schema_changes.

VONT_variable_ontology.label: Rented agricultural land share

VONT_variable_ontology.label: Geological substrate (lithology)
