peer_reviewed.Spatial_Method: MaxEnt-based climate envelope modelling to map future biome stability; standard multivariate GIS multi-criteria overlay of equally weighted layers for water infrastructure, biodiversity priorities, climate-resilient natural features, and socio-economic vulnerability to produce EbA priority areas.
peer_reviewed.Method_Validation: MaxEnt model validation against current biome distributions (hindcasting) with 86.02% accuracy.
peer_reviewed.Tool: MaxEnt (Elith J, Phillips SJ, Hastie T, Dudík M, Chee Y, Yates CJ. A statistical explanation of MaxEnt for ecologists. Divers Distributions. 2010; Warren DL, Seifert SN. Ecological niche modeling in MaxEnt: the importance of model complexity and the performance of model selection criteria. Ecol Appl. 2011; Fourcade Y, Engler JO, Rodder D, Secondi J. Mapping species distributions with MAXENT using a geographically biased sample of presence data: A performance assessment of methods for correcting sampling bias. PLoS One. 2014)
peer_reviewed.Geographic_Scope: Sub-national: Namakwa District Municipality; Alfred Nzo District Municipality; South Africa
peer_reviewed.Output: Biome stability map (projected biome climatic-envelope stability: more stable / less stable under medium- and long-term scenarios); EbA priority areas map (integrated EbA suitability/priority for implementation: high to low)

peer_reviewed.oa_title: Mapping wetland loss and restoration potential in Flanders (Belgium): an ecosystem service perspective.
peer_reviewed.NbS_Subpractice: Floodplain grasslands and forests restoration; Tidal marsh restoration
peer_reviewed.Input_Variables: Flemish Soil Map (ALBON 2014); Biological Valuation Map (Vriens et al. 2011, INBO 2015); Flood Hazard Map of 2014 (VMM 2014b); POTNAT model habitat maps (Wouters et al. 2013); Tidal marsh maps (De Keersmaeker et al. 2001); Historic forest cover (De Keersmaeker et al. 2001); Current land use maps (De Keersmaeker et al. 2001)
peer_reviewed.Spatial_Method: GIS-based multi-layer overlay of historical/current wetland maps with soil, drainage, flood-hazard, and POTNAT restoration‑potential maps rasterized to 20×20 m, normalizing indicators to 0–100 and combining two restoration-scenario overlays to derive changes in ecosystem-service supply and identify priority restoration areas; multi-scale Topographic Position Index (TPI) corrected for soil permeability to generate a seepage intensity map for denitrification-related assessments.
peer_reviewed.Method_Validation: Not adressed in paper
peer_reviewed.Tool: POTNAT model (Wouters et al. 2013); Topographic Position Index (TPI) extension for ArcView 3.x (tpi_jen.avx) (Jenness 2006)
peer_reviewed.Output: Wetland restoration opportunity map (open vs forested scenarios); Soil organic carbon stock / climate regulation map (spatial SOC and scenario change); Water quality regulation map (spatial avoided nitrate leaching / denitrification); Flood risk regulation map (flood-hazard / scenario benefit for flood reduction)

peer_reviewed.oa_title: A prioritization protocol for coastal wetland restoration on Molokaʻi, Hawaiʻi
peer_reviewed.Input_Variables: Depth to groundwater (Rapid field assessment); Groundwater salinity (Rapid field assessment); Groundwater discharge rate / springs mauka (Stearns & Macdonald 1947); Distance to nearest upland road (rapid field assessment); Elevation relative to mean sea level (Molokaʻi DEM; Sweet et al. 2022); Sea-level rise projection 2050 (Sweet et al. 2022); Sea-level rise projection 2100 (Sweet et al. 2022); Flat land availability (GIS); Soil texture (Kealia silt loam; Soil Survey Staff 2021); Soil organic carbon content (% OC) (Costech ECS 4010 CHNS/O; EPA Method 440.0 modified); Proportion native wetland plant species (rapid field assessment; Table 4; Supplementary Table S11); Native wetland plant species richness (rapid field assessment; Table 4; Supplementary Table S11); Bird habitat value for threatened/endangered waterbirds and migratory birds (rapid assessment; Supplementary Table S11); Average T&E bird count (Molokaʻi 2010–2020 dataset); Species richness of all T&E/migratory birds (Molokaʻi 2010–2020 dataset); Community support ranking (Pūlama Lima; Molokaʻi Wetland Partnership); Restoration status (rapid assessment); Area of site (Table 1); Proximity to other wetlands (Table 3); Hazards (unfenced roads, powerlines, contamination) (Table 3)
peer_reviewed.Spatial_Method: GIS-based site suitability analysis in ArcGIS Pro using a weighted multi-criteria approach across six sub-models (hydrology, resilience to sea-level rise, soils, vegetation, bird habitat value, community support), with all data rasterized to 3 m, a multiplier weighting scheme to combine criteria, and three weighting runs (equal, SLR+community 2×, SLR+community 3×) to rank the 12 Molokaʻi coastal wetlands.
peer_reviewed.Method_Validation: Not adressed in paper
peer_reviewed.Output: Wetland restoration suitability map (equal-weighted sub-models; priority classes); Wetland restoration suitability map (SLR & community-support weighted 2x; priority classes); Wetland restoration suitability map (SLR & community-support weighted 3x; priority classes)

peer_reviewed.oa_title: High-resolution wetness index mapping: A useful tool for regional scale wetland management
peer_reviewed.Input_Variables: Digital Elevation Model (Environment Agency LiDAR-derived DTMs); Slope (DEM-derived); Flow Direction (DEM-derived; TauDEM); Flow Accumulation (DEM-derived; TauDEM); Up-slope contributing area α (DEM-derived; TauDEM); Water Retention Layer (Soils NSM; LCM2007); Soils (NSM); Land Cover (LCM2007); Drainage potential (Soils NSM; LCM2007)
peer_reviewed.Spatial_Method: CTI-based wetness mapping: compute Compound Topographic Index from a 4 m LiDAR DEM using flow direction (D8) and flow accumulation to derive upslope contributing area and slope, apply CTI = ln(alpha/tan beta), aggregate drainage potential from soils and land cover to form a water-retention layer, and identify high-CTI areas as wetland restoration priorities.
peer_reviewed.Method_Validation: PHI-based validation using the Priority Habitat Inventory as reference wetland classes; statistical validation with ANOVA and post-hoc Tukey’s HSD comparing CTI values between wetland classes and non-wetland samples.
peer_reviewed.Geographic_Scope: Sub-national: Northwest England;Sub-national: Greater Manchester;National: United Kingdom;Local: Carrington Moss;Local: Risley Moss;Local: Wigan Flashes
peer_reviewed.Output: Compound Topographic Index (CTI) wetness-potential suitability map (percentile classes 1–10)

peer_reviewed.oa_title: Seas the opportunity: multi-criteria decision analysis to identify and prioritise blue carbon wetland restoration sites
peer_reviewed.Input_Variables: Maximum flooding extent (Cooper 2003; Rotzoll and Fletcher 2013; Yunus et al. 2016); Present-day inundation extent (Cooper 2003; Rotzoll and Fletcher 2013; Yunus et al. 2016); Inundation extent 2050 (Cooper 2003; Rotzoll and Fletcher 2013; Yunus et al. 2016); Inundation extent 2100 (Cooper 2003; Rotzoll and Fletcher 2013; Yunus et al. 2016); Potential tidal blockages (Howie et al. 2024); Land-use (Supplementary Material, Table 1); Land tenure (Supplementary Material, Table 1); Cadastral parcels (Supplementary Material, Table 1); LiDAR DEM (South Australian Department of Environment and Water Coastal Saltmarsh Monitoring Program, personal communication); Elevation bands mapped to vegetation types (South Australian Department of Environment and Water Coastal Saltmarsh Monitoring Program, personal communication); Pre-intervention relative carbon values by vegetation type (Lovelock 2021; Russell et al. 2024; expert elicitation); Post-intervention relative carbon values by vegetation type (Lovelock 2021; Russell et al. 2024; expert elicitation)
peer_reviewed.Spatial_Method: GIS-based spatial MCDA integrating tidal inundation under present-day and sea-level rise scenarios (bathtub model), identification of tidal blockages (point and linear intersections), and layers of land-use, land tenure, cadastral parcels and patch size to compute project-complexity, carbon sequestration potential, and blockage scores and hence identify and rank priority blue-carbon restoration sites.
peer_reviewed.Method_Validation: Qualitative verification of tidal blockages using aerial imagery, local knowledge and on-ground imagery, with a binary Yes/No verification and field verification for sites not assessable from imagery.
peer_reviewed.Tool: BlueCAM (Lovelock et al., 2022; Clean Energy Regulator, 2022) ; Tidal Reconnection Method (Clean Energy Regulator, 2022)
peer_reviewed.Output: Blue carbon project suitability/prioritisation map (scores 1–5; 64 sites; 32 high-priority ≥3); Post-intervention spatial carbon sequestration potential map (relative carbon values; scenarios 2020, 2050, 2100)

peer_reviewed.oa_title: Ecological risk assessment of wetland ecosystems using Multi Criteria Decision Making and Geographic Information System
peer_reviewed.Input_Variables: Change in natural habitats (land-use map); Water pollution (source pollution data and entrance points to the wetland); Over exploitation of natural resources (buffer extension GIS map); Salinity of wetland water (electrical conductivity data); High temperature and high evaporation (temperature and evaporation data; water depth in freshwater zone used as index)
peer_reviewed.Spatial_Method: A GIS-based, multi-criteria decision analysis; weights derived from Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP); standardized risk-layer maps are combined with a Weighted Linear Combination (Raster Calculator) to produce a final risk zoning map identifying priority areas.
peer_reviewed.Method_Validation: Sensitivity analysis (weight variation and layer elimination); AHP consistency ratio check.
peer_reviewed.Output: Ecological risk‑zoning map for Shadegan Wetland (very low, low, moderate, high, very high); Change in natural habitat risk map (very low–very high); Water pollution (wastewater/oil) risk map (very low–very high); Over‑exploitation of natural resources risk map (very low–very high); Salinity of wetland water risk map (very low–very high)

peer_reviewed.oa_title: Using Structured Decision Making to Evaluate Wetland Restoration Opportunities in the Chesapeake Bay Watershed
peer_reviewed.Input_Variables: Total nitrogen (kilograms/yr) (Chesapeake Scenario Assessment Tool); Total phosphorus (kilograms/yr) (Chesapeake Scenario Assessment Tool); Agricultural Proportion of contributing land use area (percent) (Chesapeake Conservancy, 2013/2014 High-Resolution Land Use Dataset; USDA Cropland Data Layer, 2019); Wetland function Proportion of ecoydrologically active area (percent) (The Nature Conservancy); Floodplain presence (Yes/No) (Flood Emergency Management Agency, 100-Year Effective Floodplain); Hydrologic soil groups with high storage potential and residence time proportion (percent) (Natural Resources Conservation Service SSURGO); Climate flow above-average land proportion (percent) (Anderson et al. 2016); Ecohydrologically active areas (EHA) (LiDAR-derived high-resolution topography data); Water storage capacity (WS) proportion of land designated hydrologic soil groups with high storage potential and residence time (percent) (SSURGO/NRCS)
peer_reviewed.Spatial_Method: Spatial delineation of 964 potential restoration units by LiDAR-based ecohydrologically active areas combined with land-use patches and watershed/floodplain criteria (spatial opportunity mapping); multi-criteria ranking using compensatory value function and compromise programming; principal component analysis to identify priority regions.
peer_reviewed.Method_Validation: Sensitivity analysis (varying nutrient load data, ranking methods, and criteria weights)
peer_reviewed.Output: Potential wetland restoration alternatives (polygons) [964 alternatives]; Priority restoration alternatives map (2025 nutrient-loading scenario) [184 priority alternatives; 7 regions]; Priority restoration alternatives map (2019 nutrient-loading scenario) [180 priority alternatives; 7 regions]

peer_reviewed.oa_title: A multi-Criteria Wetland Suitability Index for Restoration across Ontario’s Mixedwood Plains
peer_reviewed.Input_Variables: Agriculture (Agricultural Resource Inventory (ARI)—Ontario Ministry of Agriculture, Food, and Rural Affairs; 1983–2010); Built-up Area (Ontario Ministry of Natural Resources—OMNR; 2013); Roads (Major Roads Network—DMTI Spatial; 2018); Soil Drainage (Canadian Land Inventory—Detailed Soil Survey (DSS) Compilations; 2010); Groundwater Level (Provincial Groundwater Monitoring Network (PGMN)—Ministry of Environment; 2016); Slope (DEM90 Digital Elevation Model—EarthEnv; 2014); Stream Proximity (Stream Network—OHN; 2010); Major Waterbodies (Waterbodies Region—DMTI Spatial; 2018); DEM (DEM90 Digital Elevation Model—EarthEnv; 2014)
peer_reviewed.Spatial_Method: GIS-based multi-criteria decision analysis: seven criteria (agriculture, built-up, soil drainage, groundwater, slope, stream proximity, major waterbodies as constraint) are normalized to 0–10 and weighted by Analytical Hierarchy Process to produce a weighted Wetland Suitability Index (WSI) raster; groundwater depth interpolated by kriging to a continuous layer; best sites identified as high-WSI cells; Getis-Ord Gi* (and General G) hotspot analysis used to locate statistically significant clusters of high-suitability areas.
peer_reviewed.Method_Validation: AHP consistency ratio check (CR = 0.0771 < 0.1); overlay validation using Jaccard similarity between WSI best sites and historic wetlands (Jaccard = 0.231) and between WSI best sites and existing wetlands (Jaccard = 0.028)
peer_reviewed.Output: Wetland Suitability Index (WSI) map [continuous 0.00–9.999]; WSI "Best sites" map [WSI > 8.35]; Hot‑spot priority maps (Getis‑Ord Gi*) [census subdivisions & quaternary watersheds; hot spots 90–99%]; Overlay map of WSI best sites with presettlement wetlands (Ducks Unlimited Canada) [intersection]; Overlay map of WSI best sites with existing wetlands (Land Information Ontario) [intersection]

peer_reviewed.oa_title: Modeling the wetland restorability based on natural and anthropogenic impacts in Sanjiang Plain, China
peer_reviewed.Input_Variables: Topographic saturation (National Fundamental Geographic Information System); Soil characteristics (Soils); Stream order (DEM data); Overland flow length (Flow length module in ArcGIS from DEM); Land use (Data Center for Resources and Environmental Sciences, Chinese Academy of Sciences); Reclamation history (Data Center for Resources and Environmental Sciences, Chinese Academy of Sciences); Grain yield density (County statistical yearbooks); Power density of agricultural machinery (County statistical yearbooks)
peer_reviewed.Spatial_Method: A GIS-based multi-criteria evaluation (MCE) using PCA-derived weights to combine eight natural and anthropogenic criteria into a Restorability Index (RESI) via a linear-weighted sum; rasterized factor layers are standardized, summed in ArcGIS to produce RESI maps, then RESI values are classified with Jenks natural breaks to identify priority/restoration areas.
peer_reviewed.Method_Validation: Sensitivity analysis
peer_reviewed.Output: Restorability Index suitability map (RESI classes 1–5); County-level restoration priority map (Grades I–III)

peer_reviewed.oa_title: Saving soil carbon, greenhouse gas emissions, biodiversity and the economy: paludiculture as sustainable land use option in German fen peatlands
peer_reviewed.NbS_Subpractice: wet meadow paludiculture; wet pasture paludiculture; common reed paludiculture; cattail paludiculture
peer_reviewed.Input_Variables: Peatland/organic soil distribution (Tegetmeyer et al. 2020); Agricultural land use (Tegetmeyer et al. 2020); Protected areas status (Nerger & Zeitz 2021); Soil moisture class (SMC) (Petersen 1952; Koska 2001; Joosten et al. 2015)
peer_reviewed.Spatial_Method: A GIS-based spatial overlay and classification approach: organic soils were clipped to agricultural land, protected-area status was linked to the polygons, and four paludiculture eligibility classes were assigned to produce suitability maps (using QGIS and ArcMap).
peer_reviewed.Method_Validation: Expert review or stakeholder validation
peer_reviewed.Geographic_Scope: National: Germany; Sub-national: Mecklenburg-Vorpommern; Brandenburg; Lower Saxony; Schleswig-Holstein
peer_reviewed.Output: Paludiculture suitability/eligibility maps for agriculturally used peatlands in four North‑German federal states (classes 1–4: any paludiculture; permanent grassland or cropping with admin check; only permanent grassland with admin check; ineligible)

peer_reviewed.oa_title: Modeling the suitability of wetland restoration potential at the watershed scale
peer_reviewed.Input_Variables: Hydric soils (NRCS MUIR); Land use type (Landsat TM 1994); Slope (DEM-derived) (1:24,000-scale DEM/ DL G); Overland flow length (DEM-derived flow path) (DEM); Strahler stream order (drainage network derived from DEM) (DEM); Saturation index (without permeability) (TOPMODEL-based calculation using α and β from DEM); Saturation index (with permeability) (TOPMODEL-based calculation using α, β and soil permeability from NRCS MUIR); Soil permeability (NRCS MUIR); PDA (Proportion of stream miles meeting Ohio aquatic life-use standards) (Ohio stream water quality standards); Land use constraint (Urban, water, or transportation) (Landsat TM 1994)
peer_reviewed.Spatial_Method: GIS-based multi-criteria evaluation using a linear-weighted summation (weighted sum) of binary constraints (hydric soils; land use) and continuous factors (stream order; flow distance to streams; PDA attainment; topographic saturation index with and without soil permeability) to produce restoration-potential maps; hydrologic modeling using flow accumulation and the topographic saturation index to derive hydro-criteria; a two-phase site identification and prioritization workflow.
peer_reviewed.Method_Validation: Sensitivity analysis (alternative weights variation; transmissivity variation); Consistency validation across model runs / cross-model comparison of RP classifications (agreement percentages, e.g., base vs transmissivity variation 87.4%).
peer_reviewed.Output: Wetland restoration suitability map — Base model [0–15 none; 15–30 low; 30–45 fair; 45–60 good; 60–100 high]; Wetland restoration suitability map — Alternative weights scenario [0–15 none; 15–30 low; 30–45 fair; 45–60 good; 60–100 high]; Wetland restoration suitability map — Transmissivity variation [0–15 none; 15–30 low; 30–45 fair; 45–60 good; 60–100 high]

peer_reviewed.oa_title: Identification of wetland conservation and restoration priorities in regions of oil extraction in the Yellow River Delta using circuit theory modelling
peer_reviewed.Input_Variables: Land use/land cover (Landsat TM 1991; Landsat TM 2014; GF-1 2019); Oil wells (GF-1 2019); Rivers and water bodies (not specified); Roads (not specified); Ditches (not specified); Soil types (not specified); DEM (not specified)
peer_reviewed.Spatial_Method: GIS-based circuit theory modeling (Circuitscape) to generate resistance surfaces and electric current density; identification of corridors, pinch points and barriers via least-cost path/flow analysis; and Linkage Mapper to delineate corridors and ecological nodes for prioritization.
peer_reviewed.Method_Validation: Field verification/ground-truthing of land-use classification accuracy (>80% against field points); validation of oil-well delineation by random sampling of 150 wells achieving >99.9% precision.
peer_reviewed.Output: Potential corridors (Least Cost Paths, with/without oil wells); Pinch areas (pinch points promoting species movement, with/without oil wells); Barrier areas (areas containing barriers to movement — restoration priority, with/without oil wells); Electric current density map (spatial current density of species movement by wetland type, with/without oil wells)

peer_reviewed.oa_title: Dynamic monitoring and restorability evaluation of alpine wetland in the eastern edge of Qinghai–Tibet Plateau
peer_reviewed.Input_Variables: Slope (DEM); TWI (DEM); Stream network density (Hydro network data); Water abundance (Landsat SMA); Soil type (HWSD); Soil erodibility (Institute of Soil Science, CAS); Soil abundance (Landsat SMA); Relative humidity (Meteorological CMA/remote sensing); Vegetation type (Chinese vegetation type map); Vegetation abundance (Landsat SMA); Habitat suitability of black-necked crane (Fang et al., 2020); Village interference (Land use RESDC); Road interference (Road network data); Land use type (RESDC land use)
peer_reviewed.Spatial_Method: Linear spectral mixture analysis to quantify water, vegetation and soil abundances from Landsat data; Change Vector Analysis to quantify multi-temporal changes in wetland elements; a precedence-chart weighting approach to compute the restorability index (DLRW) from multiple factors; a Bayesian network model to map restorability levels and delineate spatial priority zones for restoration.
peer_reviewed.Method_Validation: RMSE evaluation for spectral mixture analysis (overall RMSE ≤0.026; 95% RMSE ≤0.058; majority of pixels RMSE <0.1); Bayesian network model validation via confusion matrix and error ratio using 3000 random validation points (error ratio 25.61%).
peer_reviewed.Geographic_Scope: Local: Zoige County;Zoige Wetland National Nature Reserve;Sub-national: Sichuan Province;National: China
peer_reviewed.Output: Alpine wetland restorability/suitability map (unsuitable; slightly unsuitable; less suitable; slightly suitable; suitable); Spatial distribution of key state subsets (key factor state combinations linked to restorability classes: unsuitable; slightly unsuitable; less suitable; slightly suitable; suitable); Restoration priority / optimal restorability map (primary optimisation zone; secondary optimisation zone; tertiary optimisation zone)

peer_reviewed.oa_title: Land Evaluation in terms of Agroforestry Suitability, an Approach to Improve Livelihood and Reduce Poverty: A FAO based Methodology by Geospatial Solution: A case study of Palamu district, Jharkhand, India
peer_reviewed.Input_Variables: Nutrient availability (soil data); Slope (ASTER DEM); Wetness (Landsat data); Rainfall (decadal annual rainfall data); Elevation (ASTER DEM)
peer_reviewed.Spatial_Method: Geospatial analysis using remote-sensing inputs (Landsat, ASTER DEM, decadal rainfall) to derive wetness, LULC, elevation, slope and rainfall, followed by a weighted multi-criteria overlay in ArcGIS 10.1 to produce nutrient availability and agroforestry suitability maps on 1 km grids, with proximity analyses to poverty, drainage and watershed used to identify priority areas.
peer_reviewed.Method_Validation: Proximity-based validation (proximity analysis to poverty, drainage and watershed)
peer_reviewed.Output: Agroforestry suitability map (high; medium; low; 1 km × 1 km grids)

peer_reviewed.oa_title: Assessment of remote sensing and GIS application in identification of land suitability for agroforestry: A case study of Samastipur, Bihar, India
peer_reviewed.Input_Variables: Soil nitrogen map (Indian Institute of Soil Science, Bhopal); Soil phosphorus map (Indian Institute of Soil Science, Bhopal); Soil potassium map (Indian Institute of Soil Science, Bhopal); Soil organic carbon map (Indian Institute of Soil Science, Bhopal); Soil pH map (Indian Institute of Soil Science, Bhopal); Soil fertility status map (Indian Institute of Soil Science, Bhopal); Slope (ASTER DEM) (ASTER DEM); Wetness map (Landsat-8); NDVI map (Sentinel); LULC map (Sentinel); Temperature anomaly 2050 (NCAR GIS Program, RCP6)
peer_reviewed.Spatial_Method: A GIS-based multi-criteria decision analysis using weighted overlay, where themes such as soil fertility, slope, wetness and NDVI are assigned weights via pairwise comparison and combined to produce agroforestry suitability classes.
peer_reviewed.Method_Validation: Cross-evaluation with land use/land cover (LULC) categories.
peer_reviewed.Geographic_Scope: Sub-national: Samastipur district; Bihar | National: India
peer_reviewed.Output: Agroforestry suitability map (very high; high; moderate; low)

peer_reviewed.oa_title: A GIS-based database management application for agroforestry planning and tree selection
peer_reviewed.NbS_Subpractice: Alley cropping; Shelterbelts or windbreaks; Silvopastoral; Riparian buffers; Forest farming
peer_reviewed.Input_Variables: Annual precipitation (Southeast Regional Climate Center); Minimum temperature (Southeast Regional Climate Center); Growing season (Southeast Regional Climate Center); Map unit ID (Florida Geographic Data Library, Geoplan, University of Florida); Soil composition (Florida Geographic Data Library, Geoplan, University of Florida); Soil texture (Florida Geographic Data Library, Geoplan, University of Florida); Soil drainage (Florida Geographic Data Library, Geoplan, University of Florida); Soil flooding (Florida Geographic Data Library, Geoplan, University of Florida); Soil hydric (Florida Geographic Data Library, Geoplan, University of Florida); Soil salinity (Florida Geographic Data Library, Geoplan, University of Florida); Minimum pH (Florida Geographic Data Library, Geoplan, University of Florida); Maximum pH (Florida Geographic Data Library, Geoplan, University of Florida)
peer_reviewed.Spatial_Method: GIS-based overlay of climate and soils spatial layers to derive site parameters; runtime SQL queries against a plant database to identify species adapted to those parameters.
peer_reviewed.Method_Validation: Expert review or stakeholder validation; Tested by agroforestry and forestry cooperative extension agents and planned evaluation by county extension agents, landowners and farmers in Florida.
peer_reviewed.Output: NA

peer_reviewed.oa_title: Land Suitability Evaluation for Agroforestry Using Geospatial Techniques in Genale Sub-basin Oromia, Ethiopia
peer_reviewed.Input_Variables: Soil Nitrogen (Oromia Rural Land Administration and Use Bureau soil laboratory data); Soil Potassium (Oromia Rural Land Administration and Use Bureau soil laboratory data); Soil Organic Carbon (Oromia Rural Land Administration and Use Bureau soil laboratory data); Soil Phosphorus (Oromia Rural Land Administration and Use Bureau soil laboratory data); Soil pH (Oromia Rural Land Administration and Use Bureau soil laboratory data); Mean annual rainfall (National Meteorological Agency of Ethiopia); Elevation (ASTER DEM); Slope (ASTER DEM); NDWI (Landsat 8/9)
peer_reviewed.Spatial_Method: GIS-based weighted overlay; RS-derived layers (NDWI/wetness) and thematic layers of elevation, slope, rainfall, and soil nutrients, with weights computed via Analytical Hierarchical Process (AHP) to produce a classified agroforestry suitability map (not suitable, less suitable, suitable, highly suitable).
peer_reviewed.Method_Validation: Field verification; Comparison with previously conducted field assessments/known NbS sites using Google Earth imagery; AHP consistency ratio check (CR = 0.05 < 0.1)
peer_reviewed.Geographic_Scope: Sub-national: Genale Sub-basin; Oromia Region; Bale Zone; Guji Zone | National: Ethiopia
peer_reviewed.Output: Agroforestry land suitability map (Highly suitable; Suitable; Less suitable; Not suitable)

peer_reviewed.oa_title: Integrated Analytical Hierarchical Process and Geographical Information System for Allocation of Compatible Land Uses along Uluguru Mountain Slopes
peer_reviewed.Input_Variables: Digital elevation model (https://earthexplorer.usgs.gov/); Soil map units (Msanya et al., 2001); Temperature (WorldClim) (https://www.worldclim.org/data/index.html); Rainfall (CHIRPS) (Funk et al., 2015); Soil Moisture Index (Saha et al., 2018); Land Surface Temperature (MODIS) (https://earthexplorer.usgs.gov/); Soil Properties (Msanya, 2001); Elevation (Digital elevation model) (https://earthexplorer.usgs.gov/); Slope (Digital elevation model) (https://earthexplorer.usgs.gov/)
peer_reviewed.Spatial_Method: GIS-based land-resource mapping using elevation-derived land mapping units; Analytical Hierarchical Process (AHP) to weight criteria from expert judgments; Weighted Overlay analysis in GIS to generate land-use suitability maps.
peer_reviewed.Method_Validation: Ground-truthing / field verification of land-use classification using GPS and Google Earth; Consistency Ratio check of AHP pairwise comparisons (CR ≤ 0.1)
peer_reviewed.Geographic_Scope: Local: Uluguru Mountain slopes; Morogoro urban area; Tanzania
peer_reviewed.Output: Compatible land use allocation map (settlement; conservation agriculture; agroforestry; forest)

peer_reviewed.oa_title: Place-based landscape services and potential of participatory spatial planning in multifunctional rural landscapes in Southern highlands, Tanzania
peer_reviewed.Input_Variables: Landscape service benefits points (survey mapping data); Distance from home to landscape service benefits points (survey data); Kernel density surfaces of landscape service benefits (mapped landscape service benefits points); Nearest neighbor statistics of landscape service benefits (mapped landscape service benefits points); Moran's I analysis distances (Incremental Spatial Autocorrelation) (mapped landscape service benefits points); Satellite image map (Google Earth mosaic 2012–2014 high-resolution images); Minimum bounding convex hull of landscape service points (mapped data)
peer_reviewed.Spatial_Method: Spatial analysis identified priority areas by using GIS-based methods: nearest-neighbour analysis to detect clustering of mapped landscape-service sites; kernel-density estimation to identify hotspots via spatial intensity; Incremental Spatial Autocorrelation to determine Moran’s I at multiple distances, with study areas defined by the minimum bounding convex hull around service points.
peer_reviewed.Method_Validation: Not adressed in paper
peer_reviewed.Geographic_Scope: Local: Iboya; Lulanzi; Tungamalenga; Southern Highlands; Tanzania
peer_reviewed.Output: Kernel density hotspot map of provisioning landscape services (provisioning intensity); Kernel density hotspot map of cultural landscape services (cultural intensity); Kernel density map of fresh water sources (water-source density); Kernel density map of cultivation fields (cultivation intensity)

peer_reviewed.oa_title: Use of multi-criteria analysis (MCA) for supporting community forest management
peer_reviewed.Input_Variables: I1.1 Payment of applicable taxes and fees (Khadka & Vacik 2012); I1.2 Respect for and enforcement of national and local laws (Khadka & Vacik 2012); I1.3 Evaluation of conflicting laws (Khadka & Vacik 2012); I2.5 Promotion of the plantation area (Khadka & Vacik 2012); I2.6 Development and layout of plantations (Khadka & Vacik 2012); I2.7 Control of encroachment and grazing (Khadka & Vacik 2012); I2.8 Control of forest fire and minimization of forest damage (Khadka & Vacik 2012); I2.9 Promotion of use of suitable tree species (Khadka & Vacik 2012); I3.1 Maintain ecosystem diversity (Khadka & Vacik 2012); I3.2 Native species diversity (Khadka & Vacik 2012); I3.3 Ecological assessment and diagnosis (Khadka & Vacik 2012); I4.3 Revision and timeliness of management plan (Khadka & Vacik 2012); I4.4 Review/approval of plan (Khadka & Vacik 2012); I4.5 Develop management guidelines of enterprise development and other management plans (Khadka & Vacik 2012); I4.6 Public availability of management plan (Khadka & Vacik 2012); I4.7 Knowledge of forest resource use and management plan (Khadka & Vacik 2012); I5.1 Promote participatory decision-making and social inclusion (Khadka & Vacik 2012); I5.2 Ensure transparency (Khadka & Vacik 2012); I5.3 Ensure collaboration within and among stakeholders (Khadka & Vacik 2012); I5.4 Staff safety and health (Khadka & Vacik 2012); I5.5 Special provision for unprivileged groups (Khadka & Vacik 2012); I5.6 Encourage local processing and new markets (Khadka & Vacik 2012); I5.7 Sharing in economic benefits of forest use (Khadka & Vacik 2012); I5.8 Income-generation activities (Khadka & Vacik 2012); I5.9 Use of trained and experienced persons (Khadka & Vacik 2012); I6.1 Monitoring requirement (Khadka & Vacik 2012); I6.2 Frequency and intensity of monitoring (Khadka & Vacik 2012); I6.3 Data collection and use (Khadka & Vacik 2012); I6.4 Incorporation of monitoring results into management plan (Khadka & Vacik 2012); I6.5 Availability of monitoring results (Khadka & Vacik 2012)
peer_reviewed.Spatial_Method: Multi-Criteria Analysis (MCA) using Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP) with rating, ranking, and pairwise comparisons to elicit stakeholder preferences and derive priority management strategies.

peer_reviewed.Output: NA

peer_reviewed.oa_title: Identifying Feasible Locations for Wetland Creation or Restoration in Catchments by Suitability Modelling Using Light Detection and Ranging (LiDAR) Digital Elevation Model (DEM)

peer_reviewed.Spatial_Method: GIS-based multi-criteria evaluation (MCE) with linear-weighted summation of standardized slope, topographic wetness index (TWI), distance to streams, and distance to infrastructure; LiDAR-derived terrain analysis (D8 flow direction/accumulation, Strahler stream order) to delineate streams and contributing areas for suitability mapping.

peer_reviewed.Spatial_Method: Matter-element analysis-based land suitability assessment implemented in GIS; indicator weights determined by Analytic Hierarchy Process; final suitability classes derived from distances to classical/segment fields and comprehensive correlation; overlay with 2018 land-use data to exclude immovable land uses.

peer_reviewed.oa_title: Protected areas and forest fragmentation: sustainability index for prioritizing fragments for landscape restoration

peer_reviewed.oa_title: Identifying suitable multifunctional restoration areas for Forest Landscape Restoration in Central Chile
peer_reviewed.Input_Variables: Elevation (IGM 1990); Slope (IGM 1990); Cosine of Aspect (IGM 1990); Sine of Aspect (IGM 1990); Potential Insolation (IGM 1990); Distance from Rivers (IGM 1990); Topographic Wetness Index (TWI) (IGM 1990); Topographic Position Index (TPI) (Guisan et al. 1999); Bioclimatic variables (WorldClim; Hijmans et al. 2005); Distance to Cities >20,000 inhabitants (INE 2003); Distance to villages and towns <20,000 inhabitants (INE 2003); Distance to Primary Roads (IGM 1990); Distance to Secondary Roads (IGM 1990); Land Cover 1985 (Schulz et al. 2010); Land Cover 1999 (Schulz et al. 2010); Land Cover 2008 (Schulz et al. 2010); Soil Erodibility (CONAMA 2002); Rainfall Erosivity (CONAMA 2002); Streams (IGM 1990); Permanent Bareland (1985, 1999, 2008; Schulz et al. 2010); Permanent Lentic Water (2008; Schulz et al. 2010); Existing Forest (2008 Land Cover; Schulz et al. 2010); Built-up Areas (2008 Land Cover; Schulz et al. 2010)
peer_reviewed.Spatial_Method: Habitat suitability modeling (logistic regression) to predict restoration suitability and regeneration potential; spatial multi-criteria analysis (SMCE) to map multifunctional hotspots by overlaying restoration/regeneration feasibility with three potential forest functions (habitat, erosion prevention, carbon storage) and applying spatial constraints to identify priority restoration and regeneration areas.
peer_reviewed.Method_Validation: Sixfold bootstrapped internal cross-validation (sixfold bootstrapping with 10,000 bootstrap samples); ROC AUC values: restoration suitability = 0.84, regeneration potential = 0.82.
peer_reviewed.Tool: InVEST 2.5.3 and InVEST 3 (Natural Capital Project 2013) (http://www.naturalcapitalproject.org/InVEST.html); Linkage Mapper 1.0.3 (McRae and Kavanagh 2011) (www.circuitscape.org/linkagemapper); Conefor 2.6 (Saura and Tornè 2009) (www.conefor.org); GUIDOS 1.4 (Vogt 2012) (http://forest.jrc.europa.eu/download/software/guidos/)

peer_reviewed.Spatial_Method: GIS-based multicriteria evaluation (MCE) using a weighted linear combination (WLC) of standardized criteria with weights derived from Analytic Hierarchy Process (AHP), including Boolean constraints and a one-at-a-time sensitivity analysis, to produce priority maps for forest restoration; these maps are then overlaid with land-use opportunity-cost data to identify high-priority, low-cost PES sites.

peer_reviewed.Output: Priority areas for forest restoration (very low / low / medium / high / very high); Opportunity cost (reference payment) map by rural property (US$.ha−1.year−1); Priority areas for PES implementation with reference payment values by property (target areas, payments US$.year−1)

peer_reviewed.oa_title: Complex burn prioritization models as a decision-support tool for managing prescribed fires in large, heterogeneous landscapes: an example from Everglades National Park, Florida, USA
peer_reviewed.Input_Variables: TSLF in prairie ecotype (Fire perimeters 1948-2022; Smith III et al., 2015; Malone, 2019); TSLF in marsh ecotype (Fire perimeters 1948-2022; Smith III et al., 2015; Malone, 2019); TSLF in pine rocklands ecotype (Fire perimeters 1948-2022; Smith III et al., 2015; Malone, 2019); Wildland-urban interface (WUI) (ENP roads, buildings, trails, and campsite perimeters; Barrow and National Park Service Southeast Region Geospatial Resources, 2022); Protection of cultural and archeological resources (Stutts, 2014; National Register of Historic Places); Protection of recreational resources (Barrow and National Park Service Southeast Region Geospatial Resources, 2022); Protection of endangered fauna species: Cape Sable seaside sparrow (USFWS, 2023); Protection of endangered fauna species: Florida leafwing butterfly and Bartram's scrub-hairstreak (USFWS, 2023); Protection of endangered fauna species: bonneted bat (USFWS, 2023); Protection of endangered fauna species: black rail (USFWS, 2023); Protection of endangered fauna species: snail kite (USFWS, 2023); Control of invasive plant species: old world climbing fern (Ruiz et al., 2021); Control of invasive plant species: willow (Salix caroliniana) (Ruiz et al., 2021); Control of invasive plant species: paperbark tree (Melaleuca quinquenervia) (Ruiz et al., 2021); Control of invasive plant species: Australian pine (Casuarina equisetifolia) (Ruiz et al., 2021); Control of invasive plant species: cattail (Typha spp.) (Ruiz et al., 2021); Hydrologic conditions (mean surface water depth derived from surface water stages; mean depths; South Florida Water Management District, 2023; DEM USGS, 2019); Oligotrophic status (soil phosphorus concentration map; Scheidt et al., 2021; Nocentini et al., 2021b; Nocentini & Kominoski, 2021); Size of burn unit (Table 1); Complexity of burn unit (Table 1)
peer_reviewed.Spatial_Method: A GIS-based, multi-criteria spatial decision-support approach implemented in ArcGIS Pro that converts all inputs to a 50 m raster; derives hydrologic conditions by empirical Bayesian kriging of surface-water depth (and dryness from DEM), estimates oligotrophic soil phosphorus by kriging; constructs buffers around roads, buildings, trails, and cultural/archeological resources; and integrates weighted criteria (time since last fire, WUI, protection of cultural/recreational resources, endangered fauna habitats, invasive species control, hydrologic conditions, oligotrophic status, burn-unit size/complexity) into per-pixel burn-priority scores via Model Builder.
peer_reviewed.Method_Validation: 50-year simulation of prescribed-fire management under two scenarios (S1, S2) to derive simulated mean fire return intervals, then comparison of simulated FRIs to literature-based target FRIs using Pearson’s correlation (r2 = 0.88; p = 0.02).

peer_reviewed.Output: Burn prioritization score map (50 m grid) [scores 0–71]; Burn prioritization scores averaged to burn‑unit polygons [scores 6–62]; Simulated 50‑year prescribed‑fire perimeters (annual simulated burn perimeters 2023–2072)

peer_reviewed.oa_title: Integrated Scenario Planning and Multi-Criteria Decision Analysis Framework with Application to Forest Planning
peer_reviewed.NbS_Subpractice: Plant regional coniferous species (fir, spruce and pine); western larch; aspen
peer_reviewed.Input_Variables: Forest attributes (Appendix); Biogeoclimatic zones (Appendix); Severity of beetle attack (Appendix); Beetle attack data (percentage of susceptible stands) (Appendix); Model land base area (Appendix); Stand age distribution (Stands older than 40 years) (Appendix); Management strata (m) (Appendix); Yield table projections (TIPSY 4.1) (British Columbia Ministry of Forests, 2007); Merchantable volume per hectare vmpt (Yield tables from TIPSY 4.1) (British Columbia Ministry of Forests, 2007); Net revenue per hectare nvmpt (Prices by species/grade; Table A1) (Forest Ecosystems Solutions Ltd, 2008); Harvest costs per m3 hcmpt (Table A1) (Forest Ecosystems Solutions Ltd, 2008); Discount rate r (Appendix); Initial timber inventories (Appendix); Terminal timber inventories (Appendix); Area of protected forests (Environmental goals in Table 2) (Table 2); Area of old-growth forests (Environmental goals in Table 2) (Table 2); Initial tree species abundance As(x) (Workshop results; Harshaw et al., 2006; BC MoF, 2008); Target tree species abundance TAs (Harshaw et al., 2006; BC MoF, 2008)
peer_reviewed.Spatial_Method: Spatial prioritization is performed by dividing the land base into management strata defined by species, age, site class, biogeoclimatic zone, and beetle attack severity, and solving a set of scenario-specific multi-criteria linear programs (compromise programming) to determine the optimal treatment mix across strata over the planning horizon.
peer_reviewed.Method_Validation: Expert review or stakeholder validation; Workshop-based review of scenarios with stakeholders.
peer_reviewed.Tool: GAMS (Brooke et al., 2004) ; CPLEX (IBM ILOG CPLEX Optimizer; https://www.ibm.com/products/ilog-cplex-optimizer) ; TIPSY (British Columbia Ministry of Forests and Range, 2007; https://www.for.gov.bc.ca/hre/gymodels/TIPSY/index.htm)
peer_reviewed.Geographic_Scope: Local: Quesnel; Sub-national: British Columbia; National: Canada
peer_reviewed.Output: NA

peer_reviewed.oa_title: Planning
peer_reviewed.Input_Variables: Riparian vegetation (not specified)
peer_reviewed.Spatial_Method: GIS-based multi-criteria decision-making using compromise programming to integrate forest conservation priority maps with a calculated separation distance; clustering analysis to identify potential conservation areas and delineate potential new protected areas.
peer_reviewed.Method_Validation: Not adressed in paper
peer_reviewed.Output: Forest conservation priority map; Delineated potential new protected area polygons (359 ha neighboring Kinabalu Park; 4,361 ha west of the park; group of 11 polygons totaling 2,050 ha); Green-corridor linkage map between proposed protected areas and Kinabalu Park

peer_reviewed.oa_title: Wetlands Management using GIS and Multi-Criteria Evaluation Tools
peer_reviewed.Input_Variables: Urban areas (ORCA landuse map); Wetlands (ORCA landuse map); Road network (ORCA landuse map); Rivers (ORCA landuse map); Road buffers (ORCA landuse map); River buffers (ORCA landuse map)
peer_reviewed.Spatial_Method: Fuzzy logic map overlays for multi-criteria evaluation: fuzzification of raster layers, aggregation using fuzzy operators (min, max, product, gamma), and defuzzification (optional) to identify suitable/priority areas.
peer_reviewed.Method_Validation: Not adressed in paper
peer_reviewed.Tool: Multi-Criteria Evaluation Tool (MCET) (Ponnambalam, Nirupama and Venema, 2002; doi:10.14796/JWMM.R208-13)
peer_reviewed.Output: Fuzzy suitability map for development (0 = unsuitable — 1 = suitable)
